_meta.purpose: Per-state landscaping data dictionary for Phase A.2 state-vertical hub pages (front + side + back yard install focus)
_meta.sources: USDA Plant Hardiness Zones (2023 update); EPA WaterSense state turf-replacement program directories; state Departments of Agriculture pesticide regulation tiers; CAL FIRE / state forestry defensible-space rules; state contractor licensing boards; BLS OEWS May 2024 (SOC 37-3011 Landscaping & Groundskeeping Workers state means); USGS state water-use estimates; NOAA NCEI 30-year (1991-2020) state precipitation normals.

fields.annual_rainfall_inches: NOAA NCEI 30-year state-average annual precipitation (rain + snow water equivalent)
fields.drought_tier: extreme / severe / moderate / low / none — historical 30-year drought stress driving xeriscape adoption
fields.irrigation_dependency: very-high / high / moderate / low / none — share of residential landscapes requiring irrigation to thrive
fields.frost_line_inches: Design frost depth (inches) for irrigation backflow + bed prep
fields.dominant_landscape_type: turf-dominant / native-prairie / xeriscape / desert-adapted / forest-edge / coastal-mediterranean — predominant residential aesthetic
fields.wui_defensible_space_rule: required-statewide / regional / advisory / none — wildfire defensible-space cleanup mandate
fields.license_board_name: Issuing authority for residential landscape contractor licensing
fields.permit_typical: Typical permit threshold for residential landscape installation
fields.landscaper_wage_mean_hourly: BLS OEWS May 2024 state mean hourly wage for SOC 37-3011 Landscaping & Groundskeeping Workers (USD)
fields.avg_install_low: Typical low end of front-and-back-yard residential landscape install (USD)
fields.avg_install_high: Typical high end (full design + sod + irrigation + hardscape + tree install) (USD)
fields.distinctive_law: Single most distinctive state-specific landscaping, water, pesticide, or fertilizer rule
fields.climate_concern: Primary climate, water, or substrate driver of landscape design and install in this state

AL.name: Alabama
AL.license_board_name: Alabama Department of Agriculture & Industries — Landscape Architects + Landscape Designer/Contractor Registration
AL.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; ADAI Landscape Designer registration required for design work, ADAI Pesticide Applicator credential for any chemical lawn care
AL.distinctive_law: Alabama Code §2-28 makes the Alabama Department of Agriculture & Industries the only state body that licenses both landscape designers and pesticide applicators under one program — every Alabama landscape contractor applying any pesticide, fertilizer, or fungicide on residential property must hold ADAI Pesticide Applicator credentials with separate categories for ornamental, turf, mosquito, and aquatic application, with the ADAI Pesticide Inspector Division actively enforcing residential complaint inspections statewide.
AL.climate_concern: Alabama's warm-humid Gulf Coast climate from Mobile to the Tennessee Valley supports near year-round growing on warm-season Bermuda, zoysia, and centipede turf, with St. Augustine dominant on the Gulf Coast — but the state's red-clay subsoil across Birmingham, Tuscaloosa, and Montgomery requires aggressive 2-to-3-inch topsoil amendment and gypsum incorporation on every new bed prep, doubling typical bed-prep labor versus loamy-soil states and pushing Alabama landscape install costs above neighboring Tennessee and Mississippi norms.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.license_board_name: Alaska Department of Commerce — Construction Contractors Section + Department of Environmental Conservation Pesticide Program
AK.permit_typical: borough building permit not typical for landscape install; Alaska DEC Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
AK.distinctive_law: Alaska AS 18.65.082 created the most restrictive state pesticide notification regime in the country — every Alaska landscape contractor applying restricted-use pesticides or herbicides on residential property must provide 48-hour advance written notification to all adjacent property owners and post permanent signage at the application site, reflecting the 1990s subsistence-foraging lawsuits that shaped Alaska's residential pesticide framework.
AK.climate_concern: Alaska's 105-day growing season in interior climate zones plus 100-inch frost depth in Fairbanks, Bethel, and the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta forces every irrigation backflow, drip-zone manifold, and bed prep to extreme freeze-protection scope — and the South-central Anchorage Bowl combination of permafrost-adjacent soils and short summer cool-night temperatures restricts residential turf to specialty cool-season Kentucky bluegrass and creeping red fescue blends with no warm-season turf option statewide, doubling typical sod-establishment labor versus Lower 48 norms.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.license_board_name: Arizona Registrar of Contractors — license CR-21 (Landscaping & Irrigation Systems)
AZ.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install on existing yards; ROC CR-21 specialty license required on contracts above $1,000
AZ.distinctive_law: Arizona Active Management Area (AMA) statute under ARS §45-401 imposes the most restrictive residential landscape water rules in the Southwest across the Phoenix and Tucson AMAs — every new residential landscape install must comply with AMA-specific turf-area caps, Total Maximum Annual Water Allotments, and ADWR-approved low-water plant lists, with the Phoenix AMA additionally banning new ornamental turf in non-functional areas effective post-2025 ADWR rulemaking.
AZ.climate_concern: Arizona's 13-inch annual rainfall in the Phoenix and Tucson basins makes residential landscaping the largest single residential water demand statewide — Arizona Department of Water Resources estimates 50-70% of single-family residential water use goes to outdoor irrigation, driving xeriscape and ADWR-approved low-water-use plant lists as the default Phoenix-Tucson residential aesthetic, with desert-adapted Texas Ranger, Yellow Bells, Palo Verde, and Saguaro cactus replacing ornamental turf on the majority of new-build front yards.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.license_board_name: Arkansas State Plant Board — Pesticide Applicator + Arkansas Contractors Licensing Board (Residential Remodeler >$2,000)
AR.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; Arkansas State Plant Board Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
AR.distinctive_law: Arkansas Code §2-16 makes the Arkansas State Plant Board the residential pesticide applicator licensor — Arkansas requires every commercial landscape contractor applying any restricted-use pesticide on residential property to hold a State Plant Board Pesticide Applicator credential with separate categories for ornamental, turf, and right-of-way application, plus Arkansas's 1979 Right-to-Farm Act §2-4-101 limits residential nuisance complaints against neighboring agricultural pesticide drift on landscape installations adjacent to Delta row-crop operations.
AR.climate_concern: Arkansas's Ozark and Ouachita mountain landscapes combine acidic forest-soil substrates with intense seasonal pollen and pine-needle accumulation, requiring annual topdressing and pH amendment on every Bermuda, zoysia, and tall fescue turf install — and the Arkansas Delta clay substrate across the Eastern Arkansas counties from Jonesboro to Pine Bluff requires deep ripping plus 2-to-3-inch sand-and-organic amendment on every new lawn install, with backyard drainage swales mandatory in NRCS hydric soil mapping.

CA.name: California
CA.license_board_name: California Contractors State License Board — C-27 (Landscaping)
CA.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install on existing yards; CSLB C-27 license required above $500 contract; CDFA Pest Control Business License required for any pesticide application; CAL FIRE defensible-space inspection mandatory in WUI-mapped State Responsibility Area
CA.distinctive_law: California Government Code §65595 and the Model Water Efficient Landscape Ordinance (MWELO) impose the most restrictive residential landscape water rules in the country — every new California residential landscape installation greater than 500 sq ft must submit a Landscape Documentation Package showing Water Budget compliance with ETo-based plant factors, hydrozone separation, and CIMIS-verified irrigation efficiency, with cities including Los Angeles, San Diego, San Jose, Sacramento, and Fresno separately enforcing turf-replacement rebate programs and ornamental-turf removal mandates on non-functional residential turf areas.
CA.climate_concern: California's combination of multi-year drought cycles, the 2022 SGMA groundwater pumping restrictions, and CAL FIRE Tier 3 WUI defensible-space rules across the Sierra foothills, North Coast, and Santa Monica Mountains has fundamentally restructured residential landscape design statewide — drought-tolerant California-native plant communities, Mediterranean aromatic herbs, and decomposed-granite hardscape now dominate the typical Bay Area, LA, and Central Valley front-yard install, with CAL FIRE ember-resistant Zone 0 cleanup driving complete shrub removal within 5 ft of every WUI structure.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license — Colorado Department of Agriculture Pesticide Applicator + Denver, Colorado Springs, Aurora municipal contractor credentials
CO.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; CDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Aurora and Castle Rock impose drought-rule turf caps on new construction
CO.distinctive_law: Colorado HB 22-1151 (Turf Replacement Program) created the first statewide ornamental-turf removal incentive in the Rocky Mountain West — Colorado Water Conservation Board administers the $2-per-square-foot rebate fund for residential conversion of irrigated cool-season turf to xeriscape or low-water perennial beds, with the state additionally tightening rain-barrel collection rules under HB 16-1005 to limit residential rainwater harvesting to two 55-gallon barrels per single-family residence, the most restrictive residential rainwater rule in the country.
CO.climate_concern: Colorado's 17-inch state-average annual rainfall combined with 36-inch frost depth on the Front Range and the South Platte River Compact's prior-appropriation water rights doctrine forces every Denver, Boulder, Colorado Springs, and Fort Collins residential landscape install toward xeriscape, native-prairie, and short-grass perennial palettes — and Front Range hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely shred ornamental annuals, perennial flower beds, and large-leaf shrubs during the May-June hail season, driving hail-tolerant plant selection and reduced-leaf-area landscape design as standard Front Range residential practice.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.license_board_name: Connecticut Department of Energy & Environmental Protection — Pesticide Applicator + Department of Consumer Protection HIC registration
CT.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; HIC registration required above $200 contract; CT DEEP Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; municipal turf pesticide bans in 30+ towns
CT.distinctive_law: Connecticut General Statutes §22a-66l bans every pesticide application on the grounds of K-8 schools and daycare centers — and 30+ Connecticut towns including Branford, Greenwich, and West Hartford have separately adopted municipal turf-pesticide ordinances banning synthetic pesticide application on residential lawns adjacent to school property, making Connecticut the most restrictive Northeast state on residential landscape pesticide use and forcing organic IPM as the default Connecticut residential lawn-care service offering.
CT.climate_concern: Connecticut's combination of acidic forest-soil substrates across the Naugatuck and Connecticut River valleys, mature deciduous canopy across the Litchfield Hills and Fairfield County, and 50-inch annual precipitation drives lime application as a standard component of every Connecticut turf install — and the state's heavily-shaded suburban lots from Greenwich to Hartford restrict cool-season Kentucky bluegrass establishment, pushing typical Connecticut residential turf installs toward shade-tolerant fine fescue blends with mid-cycle overseeding service contracts.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.license_board_name: Delaware Division of Revenue — Business License + Delaware Department of Agriculture Pesticide Section
DE.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; Delaware Business License only; DDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
DE.distinctive_law: Delaware Department of Agriculture Pesticide Section under 3 Del. Code §1201 imposes one of the most restrictive residential pesticide-drift notification regimes in the Mid-Atlantic — every Delaware landscape contractor applying any restricted-use pesticide on residential property within 100 ft of an occupied structure must provide 24-hour advance written notification, plus Delaware Coastal Zone Act §7001 separately restricts heavy fertilizer application within the Inland Bays watershed across Sussex County to protect Rehoboth Bay and Indian River Bay water quality.
DE.climate_concern: Delaware's coastal Sussex County combination of sandy fast-draining soils and high summer humidity forces irrigation on every new turf install along the Cape Henlopen-to-Bethany corridor — and the Inland Bays nutrient-management framework restricts standard high-nitrogen turf fertilizer programs across all of Sussex County, pushing Delaware coastal landscape contractors toward slow-release organic fertilizer programs with mandatory soil-test-based application rates on every Delaware Bay watershed property.

FL.name: Florida
FL.license_board_name: Florida Department of Agriculture & Consumer Services — Limited Lawn & Ornamental Pest Control License + DBPR Construction (no separate landscape license)
FL.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install on existing yards; FDACS Limited Lawn & Ornamental Pest Control License required for any chemical lawn care; HOA approval typical in deed-restricted communities
FL.distinctive_law: Florida Statute §373.185 (Florida-Friendly Landscaping Act) protects the right of every Florida homeowner to install Florida-Friendly Landscaping (xeriscape, native plants, low-water-use vegetation) regardless of HOA covenants, restrictions, or deed-recorded landscape requirements — overriding HOA mandates in deed-restricted communities and forcing every Florida HOA architectural review committee to approve Florida-Friendly Landscaping applications meeting the FFL principles published by UF/IFAS Extension.
FL.climate_concern: Florida's combination of 365-day growing season, sandy fast-draining soils across the entire peninsula, and persistent summer humidity drives the highest residential irrigation dependency in the Southeast — and the state's St. Augustinegrass turf monoculture across single-family residential subdivisions from Jacksonville to Naples requires fungicide-rotation programs to manage take-all root rot and gray leaf spot, driving Florida residential turf-care service contracts toward 8-12 monthly visits per year versus 4-6 in moderate-climate states.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.license_board_name: Georgia Department of Agriculture — Commercial Pesticide Applicator + Georgia State Licensing Board for Residential & General Contractors
GA.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; GDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; metro Atlanta watering restrictions enforce odd-even residential irrigation schedules
GA.distinctive_law: Georgia Water Stewardship Act (OCGA §12-5-7) imposes statewide odd-even outdoor irrigation restrictions on every Georgia residential property — single-family homes may water no more than three days per week with daytime irrigation banned between 10am and 4pm year-round, making Georgia the only Southeast state with permanent statewide residential irrigation restrictions enforceable by municipal code-enforcement, with metro Atlanta water utilities additionally tiering residential water rates aggressively against high-volume outdoor irrigation users.
GA.climate_concern: Georgia's red-clay subsoil across the Piedmont from Atlanta to Athens to Macon requires aggressive 2-to-4-inch topsoil amendment and gypsum incorporation on every new bed prep — and the state's high mature-deciduous tree canopy, combined with Atlanta's 2,400+ Heating Degree Day winter and 250-day growing season, restricts residential lawn establishment to shade-tolerant fine fescue or zoysia blends in the urban canopy and full-sun Bermuda or centipede in suburban subdivision lots without mature trees.

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.license_board_name: Hawaii Department of Commerce & Consumer Affairs — Contractors License Board (C-27 Landscaping)
HI.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install on existing yards; C-27 specialty license required statewide on contracts above $1,500; Hawaii Department of Agriculture import permits required on every plant material brought from off-island
HI.distinctive_law: Hawaii HRS §150A imposes the most restrictive plant material import quarantine in the U.S. — every plant, soil amendment, or organic mulch product imported to Hawaii from the mainland or another Hawaiian island must clear Hawaii Department of Agriculture inspection at the airport or harbor, doubling typical landscape material lead times and adding 15-30% to imported plant material costs, with the program targeting coqui frog, fire ant, and rhinoceros beetle interception on every shipment.
HI.climate_concern: Hawaii's island-tropical combination of dramatic windward-vs-leeward microclimates within the same island drives radically different residential landscape design — Oahu's windward Kailua-Kaneohe corridor with 65 inches/year rainfall supports lush tropical plumeria, hibiscus, and ti palette versus the leeward Ewa Beach desert at 15 inches/year requiring desert-adapted aloe, agave, and crown flower, and every coastal exposure mandates salt-tolerant beach naupaka, sea grape, and ironwood for windbreak hedging on every windward-coastal property.

ID.name: Idaho
ID.license_board_name: Idaho Division of Occupational and Professional Licenses — Contractor Registration + Idaho State Department of Agriculture Pesticide Section
ID.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; Idaho Contractor Registration required statewide; ISDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
ID.distinctive_law: Idaho Code §22-3401 grants the Idaho State Department of Agriculture exclusive authority over residential pesticide regulation and preempts every municipal pesticide ordinance — Idaho is one of only a few states with statewide pesticide preemption explicitly blocking cities like Boise, Moscow, or Sun Valley from imposing local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, leaving the ISDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator credential as the only operational requirement statewide.
ID.climate_concern: Idaho's high-altitude combination of 130-day growing season in the Wood River Valley and Boise foothills, intense Sun Valley UV exposure above 5,000 ft elevation, and 19-inch annual rainfall supports xeriscape, native-prairie, and short-grass perennial palettes as the dominant Idaho residential landscape aesthetic — and Idaho's mountain-valley boulder substrates and decomposed-granite soils require ripping plus 2-to-4-inch topsoil amendment on every new bed prep across Boise, Coeur d'Alene, and Idaho Falls.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; Illinois Department of Agriculture Pesticide Applicator; Chicago Department of Buildings Class B contractor credentials
IL.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; IDOA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Chicago turf pesticide ban on park district properties
IL.distinctive_law: Illinois Pesticide Act (415 ILCS 60) requires every Illinois landscape contractor applying restricted-use pesticides on residential property to hold an Illinois Department of Agriculture Commercial Pesticide Applicator credential — and the Lawn Care Products Application & Notice Act (415 ILCS 65) separately requires every Illinois lawn-care company to provide written advance notification to neighbors within 50 ft of any residential pesticide application, plus mandatory yard-marker signage immediately following application, the most restrictive notification regime in the Midwest.
IL.climate_concern: Illinois's central-Midwest combination of deep prairie loam soils across the Chicago suburbs and central Illinois Black Belt, mature deciduous canopy in older Chicago neighborhoods, and 180-day growing season supports cool-season Kentucky bluegrass-perennial ryegrass-fine fescue blend turf as the dominant Illinois residential lawn — and Illinois's pre-1978 housing-stock concentration in Chicago, Rockford, and Peoria requires lead-soil testing and EPA RRP Lead-Safe Certified bed prep on any landscape install that disturbs more than 6 sq ft of pre-1978 painted exterior building face.

IN.name: Indiana
IN.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; Indiana Office of the State Chemist Pesticide Applicator; municipal contractor credentials in Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, Evansville
IN.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; OISC Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
IN.distinctive_law: Indiana Code §15-16-5 makes the Indiana Office of the State Chemist (housed at Purdue University) the residential pesticide applicator licensor — Indiana is unusual in housing pesticide regulation under a university Office of the State Chemist rather than a Department of Agriculture, with OISC operating both the licensing program and the academic Purdue Pesticide Programs continuing-education channel for every Indiana commercial landscape contractor applying chemicals on residential property statewide.
IN.climate_concern: Indiana's central-Midwest combination of glacial-till loam soils, mature deciduous canopy in Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, and Evansville older neighborhoods, and 175-day growing season supports cool-season Kentucky bluegrass-perennial ryegrass blend turf as the dominant Indiana residential lawn — and Indiana's central-state corn-pollen and soybean-dust contamination during the August-September harvest season measurably stresses ornamental flower beds and freshly-installed shade-tree saplings, driving Indiana landscape installs toward post-harvest October planting on residential subdivisions adjacent to active row-crop operations.

IA.name: Iowa
IA.license_board_name: Iowa Department of Agriculture & Land Stewardship Pesticide Bureau + Iowa Workforce Development Construction Contractor Registration
IA.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; IDALS Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Iowa Construction Contractor Registration required statewide above $2,000 annual gross
IA.distinctive_law: Iowa Administrative Code 21-45 imposes one of the most restrictive residential pesticide notification regimes in the Midwest — every Iowa commercial landscape contractor applying restricted-use pesticides on residential property must maintain a Sensitive-Crop Registry through IDALS that notifies adjacent organic, beekeeping, and certified-organic-row-crop operations 48 hours in advance of any drift-prone application, reflecting the state's Iowa Bee Sentinel Apiary Registry framework protecting commercial honey-production operations adjacent to suburban subdivisions.
IA.climate_concern: Iowa's deep prairie-loam soils across the entire state combined with 165-day growing season and 48-inch frost depth support cool-season Kentucky bluegrass turf as the dominant Iowa residential lawn — and Iowa's central-state corn-pollen surge in late July, soybean-dust contamination during the August-September harvest season, and Western Iowa wind erosion across the Loess Hills measurably stress ornamental flower beds, driving Iowa residential bed-mulch programs toward 3-inch shredded-hardwood barriers replenished annually to suppress wind-blown weed seed germination.

KS.name: Kansas
KS.license_board_name: Kansas Department of Agriculture Pesticide & Fertilizer Program; Wichita and Overland Park municipal contractor credentials
KS.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; KDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Wichita and Overland Park municipal contractor registration required
KS.distinctive_law: Kansas Statute §2-2438 grants the Kansas Department of Agriculture exclusive preemption over every residential and municipal pesticide ordinance — Kansas is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the country, explicitly blocking Wichita, Lawrence, and Kansas City KS from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, with KDA Pesticide & Fertilizer Program managing the only operational regulatory channel statewide.
KS.climate_concern: Kansas's transition from Eastern tallgrass prairie to Western shortgrass prairie creates dramatic east-west landscape design variation — Eastern Kansas Johnson County and Wichita support full Kentucky bluegrass and tall fescue cool-season turf with regular irrigation, while Western Kansas Garden City and Dodge City restrict residential turf to drought-tolerant buffalograss and blue grama native cultivars, and central-plains hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely shred ornamental flower beds and large-leaf shrubs across the entire state during the April-June severe weather season.

KY.name: Kentucky
KY.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; Kentucky Department of Agriculture Pesticide Applicator; Louisville Metro and Lexington-Fayette municipal contractor credentials
KY.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; KDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Louisville Metro contractor registration required for residential exterior contracting above $5,000
KY.distinctive_law: Kentucky Revised Statutes §217B grants the Kentucky Department of Agriculture preemption authority over municipal pesticide ordinances — Kentucky landscape contractors operate exclusively under KDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator credentialing with no city-level pesticide restrictions in Louisville, Lexington, or Bowling Green, and KDA additionally operates the Kentucky Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting beekeeping and certified-organic operations adjacent to residential subdivisions from drift-prone restricted-use pesticide applications.
KY.climate_concern: Kentucky's Bluegrass region combination of acidic forest-soil substrates across the Bluegrass and Pennyroyal regions, mature deciduous canopy in Louisville's Old Louisville and Lexington's Chevy Chase historic neighborhoods, and 195-day growing season supports cool-season tall fescue turf as the dominant Kentucky residential lawn — and Eastern Kentucky Appalachian hill-country landscape installs require terraced retaining walls and drainage swales on every sloping residential lot, doubling typical hardscape labor versus flat suburban subdivision installs in Lexington and Louisville.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.license_board_name: Louisiana Horticulture Commission — Landscape Architect, Landscape Horticulturist, Landscape Irrigation Contractor + Pesticide Applicator
LA.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; Louisiana Horticulture Commission Landscape Horticulturist license required for any commercial landscape contracting; Louisiana Horticulture Commission Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
LA.distinctive_law: Louisiana RS §3:3801 makes Louisiana one of the only states that licenses residential landscape contractors as Landscape Horticulturists through a dedicated Horticulture Commission — every Louisiana commercial landscape contractor working on residential property must hold a Landscape Horticulturist license with separate exam categories for design, installation, and maintenance, plus a Landscape Irrigation Contractor credential for any irrigation work and a Pesticide Applicator credential for any chemical application, the most fragmented residential landscape licensing scheme in the Southeast.
LA.climate_concern: Louisiana's combination of 60-inch annual rainfall concentrated in tropical-storm-driven downbursts, alluvial Mississippi River Delta soils across South Louisiana from Baton Rouge to New Orleans, and the longest residential growing season in the continental U.S. outside South Florida and South Texas drives near-year-round residential landscape demand — and the state's St. Augustinegrass turf monoculture across single-family residential subdivisions requires fungicide-rotation programs for take-all root rot and gray leaf spot, plus seasonal nutria, fire ant, and Formosan termite management on every Louisiana property.

ME.name: Maine
ME.license_board_name: Maine Board of Pesticides Control + Maine Board of Licensure of Landscape Architects
ME.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; Maine Board of Pesticides Control Master Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
ME.distinctive_law: Maine 22 MRS §1471-X (Maine Pesticides Control Act) imposes the most restrictive residential pesticide notification regime in New England — every Maine commercial landscape contractor must register with the Maine Board of Pesticides Control as a Master Applicator and provide 24-hour advance written notification to all adjacent property owners before any restricted-use pesticide application, plus Maine LD 1518 separately preempts municipal pesticide ordinances exclusively on commercial landscape contracting (residential homeowner application remains regulated by 30+ Maine municipalities).
ME.climate_concern: Maine's combination of 140-day growing season in interior climate zones 3b-5a, acidic coniferous forest-soil substrates across the entire state, and 60-inch frost depth supports forest-edge landscape design with native white pine, balsam fir, and lowbush blueberry as dominant Maine residential plant palette — and Maine's coastal salt-spray on Penobscot Bay, Casco Bay, and Mount Desert Island restricts shoreline residential landscaping to salt-tolerant beach plum, bayberry, and northern bayberry hedging, with imported topsoil + 4-inch peat amendment standard on every coastal Maine bed prep.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.license_board_name: Maryland Department of Agriculture Pesticide Regulation Section + Maryland Home Improvement Commission
MD.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; MDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; MHIC license required above $500 contract; Maryland Lawn Fertilizer Law caps phosphorus and nitrogen application statewide
MD.distinctive_law: Maryland Lawn Fertilizer Law (Agriculture Article §8-803.1) imposes the most restrictive residential turf fertilizer rule in the country — every Maryland commercial lawn-care contractor and homeowner must apply zero phosphorus on established turf (except at establishment), cap nitrogen at 0.7 lb per 1,000 sq ft per application, and prohibit fall fertilizer application after November 15, all to protect the Chesapeake Bay TMDL nutrient-reduction targets, with MDA enforcement targeting commercial landscape contractor compliance through residential complaint inspections.
MD.climate_concern: Maryland's combination of Chesapeake Bay watershed-protection nutrient caps under the Lawn Fertilizer Law, mature deciduous canopy across the Washington-Baltimore corridor and Eastern Shore, and 200-day growing season pushes Maryland residential landscape design toward bay-friendly natives, low-input cool-season tall fescue turf, and rain garden bioretention on every new install — and the state's pre-1978 housing-stock concentration in Baltimore rowhouse districts and Frederick historic core triggers lead-soil testing on landscape installs adjacent to pre-1978 painted exteriors.

MA.name: Massachusetts
MA.license_board_name: Massachusetts Department of Agricultural Resources Pesticide Program + Massachusetts Office of Consumer Affairs HIC registration
MA.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; HIC registration required above $1,000 contract; MDAR Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; municipal turf pesticide ordinances in 30+ Massachusetts towns
MA.distinctive_law: Massachusetts MGL Ch 132B §6F imposes the Massachusetts Children's & Families' Protection Act on every residential pesticide application — every Massachusetts commercial landscape contractor applying restricted-use pesticides on residential property within 75 ft of a child-occupied structure must provide 72-hour advance written notification, plus 30+ Massachusetts towns including Newton, Cambridge, Marblehead, and Wellesley have separately adopted municipal turf-pesticide ordinances banning synthetic pesticide application on residential lawns, making Massachusetts the most restrictive Northeast state on residential landscape pesticide use after Connecticut.
MA.climate_concern: Massachusetts's combination of acidic glacial-till soils across the Berkshires, Connecticut River Valley, and South Coast, mature deciduous canopy across suburban Boston from Newton to Concord to Wellesley, and 175-day growing season requires lime application as a standard component of every Massachusetts turf install — and the state's sandy outwash soils across Cape Cod, Martha's Vineyard, and Nantucket combined with restrictive Cape Cod Commission Land Use Bylaw require nitrogen-fixing native plants and reduced-fertilizer landscape design on every Cape and Islands residential property to protect coastal pond and estuary water quality.

MI.name: Michigan
MI.license_board_name: Michigan Department of Agriculture & Rural Development Pesticide Section + Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs
MI.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; MDARD Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Michigan M&A Contractor registration required above $600 contract
MI.distinctive_law: Michigan PA 451 of 1994 §324.8316 makes Michigan one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Midwest — Michigan Department of Agriculture & Rural Development holds exclusive authority over residential pesticide regulation and explicitly preempts every municipal pesticide ordinance, blocking Ann Arbor, Detroit, Grand Rapids, and Lansing from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, while MDARD operates the Michigan Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting cherry orchards, blueberry farms, and Michigan honey-production operations adjacent to suburban subdivisions.
MI.climate_concern: Michigan's combination of Great Lakes lake-effect microclimates across the Lower Peninsula, glacial-till loam soils across Kent, Oakland, and Wayne counties, and 160-day growing season supports cool-season Kentucky bluegrass-perennial ryegrass blend turf as the dominant Michigan residential lawn — and Michigan's lake-effect snow loads on Detroit, Grand Rapids, and Traverse City landscapes drive snow-shoveling-corridor design considerations on every front-yard install plus salt-tolerant plant selection on grade-level beds adjacent to Michigan Department of Transportation snow-operations corridors.

MN.name: Minnesota
MN.license_board_name: Minnesota Department of Agriculture Pesticide & Fertilizer Management + Minnesota Department of Labor & Industry Residential Building Contractor
MN.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; MDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; DLI residential contractor license required above $15,000 annual gross
MN.distinctive_law: Minnesota Statute §18B.07 (Minnesota Pollinator Protection Act) imposes one of the most restrictive residential neonicotinoid restrictions in the country — every Minnesota commercial landscape contractor must label and disclose every neonicotinoid-treated plant, seed, or soil amendment delivered to residential property, and the Minnesota Department of Agriculture separately operates the Minnesota Pollinator Habitat Registry providing rebate incentives for residential conversion of cool-season turf to pollinator-friendly native prairie meadow landscaping.
MN.climate_concern: Minnesota's combination of -30°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, 145-day growing season in interior climate zone 4a-5a, and 60-inch frost depth restricts residential turf to ultra-cold-hardy Kentucky bluegrass and creeping red fescue cool-season blends — and Minnesota's lake-effect humidity from the 10,000+ regional lakes supports lush hosta, hydrangea, and shade-perennial residential garden design while requiring aggressive fall garlic-mustard, buckthorn, and emerald-ash-borer-related ash-tree removal on every Twin Cities and Duluth landscape maintenance contract.

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.license_board_name: Mississippi Department of Agriculture & Commerce Bureau of Plant Industry — Pesticide Section + Mississippi State Board of Contractors
MS.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; MDAC Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; MSBC residential license required above $50,000 contract
MS.distinctive_law: Mississippi Code §69-23 makes the Mississippi Department of Agriculture & Commerce Bureau of Plant Industry the residential pesticide applicator licensor — and Mississippi grants MDAC exclusive preemption over every municipal pesticide ordinance, blocking Jackson, Hattiesburg, and Tupelo from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, with MDAC additionally operating the Mississippi Imported Fire Ant Quarantine that restricts soil, sod, and nursery stock movement across federally-quarantined county boundaries on every interstate plant material delivery.
MS.climate_concern: Mississippi's combination of warm-humid Gulf Coast climate from Bay St. Louis to Pascagoula, alluvial Mississippi River Delta soils across the Western Mississippi counties from Greenville to Vicksburg, and 240-day growing season supports near-year-round residential landscape demand on warm-season Bermuda, zoysia, and centipede turf — and the Mississippi pine-belt across the Southern Pine Hills from Hattiesburg to Laurel supports loblolly and longleaf pine canopy that drives fungicide-rotation programs for take-all root rot on every St. Augustinegrass install in the coastal corridor.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; Missouri Department of Agriculture Bureau of Pesticide Control; St. Louis and Kansas City municipal contractor credentials
MO.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; MDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; St. Louis Class B contractor registration for residential exterior contracting above $5,000
MO.distinctive_law: Missouri Revised Statutes §281.103 grants the Missouri Department of Agriculture exclusive preemption authority over residential and municipal pesticide ordinances — Missouri is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Midwest, blocking St. Louis, Kansas City, Columbia, and Springfield from imposing local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, with MDA Bureau of Pesticide Control managing the only operational regulatory channel statewide and MDA additionally maintaining the Missouri Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting beekeeping operations.
MO.climate_concern: Missouri's transition zone between Northern cool-season and Southern warm-season turf grasses creates one of the most variable residential landscape design environments in the Midwest — Northern Missouri Kansas City and St. Joseph support cool-season tall fescue and Kentucky bluegrass, while Southern Missouri Springfield and the Bootheel default to warm-season Bermuda and zoysia, with Central Missouri Columbia and Jefferson City requiring transition-zone overseed schedules that combine spring Bermuda greenup with fall fescue overseeding on every premium residential install.

MT.name: Montana
MT.license_board_name: Montana Department of Agriculture Pesticide Section + Montana Department of Labor & Industry Independent Contractor Registration
MT.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; MDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Montana Independent Contractor Registration required statewide (workers' compensation focus)
MT.distinctive_law: Montana Code §80-8-105 makes the Montana Department of Agriculture the residential pesticide applicator licensor and grants MDA preemption authority over every municipal pesticide ordinance — Montana is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Mountain West, blocking Bozeman, Missoula, and Billings from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, while MDA additionally operates the Montana Noxious Weed Trust Fund providing cost-share for residential property owners managing leafy spurge, knapweed, and Russian olive infestations.
MT.climate_concern: Montana's high-altitude Big Sky combination of 130-day growing season in interior climate zones 3a-5a, intense Rocky Mountain UV exposure above 4,000 ft elevation, and 16-inch annual rainfall supports xeriscape, native shortgrass-prairie, and short-grass perennial palettes as the dominant Montana residential landscape aesthetic — and the state's mountain-valley boulder substrates and decomposed-granite soils require ripping plus 2-to-4-inch topsoil amendment on every new bed prep across Bozeman, Missoula, Helena, and Whitefish residential installs.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.license_board_name: Nebraska Department of Agriculture Pesticide & Fertilizer Program + Nebraska Department of Labor Contractor Registration
NE.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; NDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Nebraska Contractor Registration required statewide above $2,500 annual gross
NE.distinctive_law: Nebraska Revised Statute §2-2641 makes the Nebraska Department of Agriculture the residential pesticide applicator licensor with statewide preemption over municipal pesticide ordinances — and Nebraska's Pollinator Protection Initiative under LB 461 (2017) requires every commercial landscape contractor applying neonicotinoid-class pesticides on residential property to label and disclose treated plant material, plus Nebraska operates the Nebraska Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting commercial honeybee operations adjacent to Lincoln, Omaha, and Grand Island residential subdivisions.
NE.climate_concern: Nebraska's transition from Eastern tallgrass prairie to Western shortgrass prairie creates dramatic east-west landscape design variation — Eastern Nebraska Lincoln-Omaha supports full cool-season Kentucky bluegrass and tall fescue residential turf with regular irrigation, while Western Nebraska Scottsbluff and Sandhills restrict residential turf to drought-tolerant buffalograss native cultivars, and Lincoln-Omaha winter chinook wind events plus central-plains hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely shred ornamental flower beds across the entire state during the April-June severe weather season.

NV.name: Nevada
NV.license_board_name: Nevada State Contractors Board — license C-10 (Landscape Contracting) + Nevada Department of Agriculture Pesticide Branch
NV.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install on existing yards; NSCB C-10 specialty license required statewide on contracts above $1,000; NDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
NV.distinctive_law: Nevada AB 356 (2021) created the most restrictive ornamental turf removal mandate in the country — every non-functional ornamental turf area within the Southern Nevada Water Authority service area (Las Vegas, Henderson, North Las Vegas, Boulder City) must be removed by 2027, with the Nevada State Contractors Board enforcing C-10 license-holder compliance on every Las Vegas Valley residential turf-replacement contract, and SNWA additionally administering a $3-per-square-foot turf-removal rebate that has converted over 200 million sq ft of Las Vegas Valley residential turf to xeriscape since 2003.
NV.climate_concern: Nevada's Mojave Desert combination of 10-inch annual rainfall in the Las Vegas Valley, extreme summer heat above 110°F driving plant transpiration losses, and Lake Mead Colorado River allocation pressure makes residential outdoor water use the largest discretionary residential water demand in the state — Las Vegas Valley xeriscape installs with desert-adapted Mojave Aster, Desert Willow, and Sonoran Palo Verde now dominate the typical front-yard install, with Northern Nevada Reno-Sparks adopting Sierra-Nevada-foothills xeriscape with native sagebrush and Great Basin grasses on every new residential install.

NH.name: New Hampshire
NH.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; New Hampshire Department of Agriculture, Markets & Food Pesticide Control Division
NH.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; NHDAMF Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
NH.distinctive_law: New Hampshire RSA 430:31 makes the New Hampshire Department of Agriculture, Markets & Food Pesticide Control Division the residential pesticide applicator licensor with statewide preemption over every municipal pesticide ordinance — Live Free or Die framework explicitly blocks Manchester, Concord, Nashua, and Portsmouth from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, while NHDAMF maintains the New Hampshire Pollinator Stewardship Initiative protecting commercial maple sugaring operations and honeybee yards adjacent to suburban subdivisions.
NH.climate_concern: New Hampshire's combination of acidic White Mountain forest-soil substrates, Granite State boulder substrates across the Lakes Region, and 150-day growing season restricts residential lawn establishment to specialty cool-season fine fescue and creeping red fescue blends with mid-cycle overseeding — and Seacoast salt-spray on Hampton, Rye, and Portsmouth residential properties combined with intense White Mountains fall-foliage maple-tannin drip on east-facing exterior elevations measurably stresses ornamental flower beds and freshly-installed shade-tree saplings.

NJ.name: New Jersey
NJ.license_board_name: New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection Pesticide Control Program + New Jersey Division of Consumer Affairs HIC registration
NJ.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; HIC registration required above $500 contract; NJ DEP Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; New Jersey Fertilizer Law caps phosphorus and nitrogen application statewide
NJ.distinctive_law: New Jersey Fertilizer Law (NJSA 58:10A-64) imposes the most restrictive residential turf fertilizer rule in the Mid-Atlantic outside Maryland — every New Jersey commercial lawn-care contractor and homeowner must apply zero phosphorus on established turf, cap nitrogen at 0.7 lb per 1,000 sq ft per application, prohibit fall fertilizer application after November 15, and post mandatory contractor signage detailing application content, all to protect Barnegat Bay and Delaware Bay TMDL nutrient-reduction targets, with NJDEP enforcement targeting commercial landscape contractor compliance through residential complaint inspections.
NJ.climate_concern: New Jersey's combination of Coastal Plain sandy soils across the Pine Barrens and Jersey Shore, dense suburban canopy across Bergen and Morris counties, and Barnegat Bay watershed nutrient-management framework restricts standard high-nitrogen turf fertilizer programs across the state — and the state's Pinelands National Reserve overlay across Atlantic, Burlington, Cumberland, Cape May, Camden, Gloucester, Ocean counties imposes Pinelands Commission-approved native plant lists on every new residential landscape install in the 1.1-million-acre overlay zone.

NM.name: New Mexico
NM.license_board_name: New Mexico Regulation & Licensing Department — Construction Industries Division (LD specialty) + New Mexico Department of Agriculture Pesticide Compliance
NM.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install on existing yards; CID LD specialty contractor license required statewide on contracts above $7,200; NMDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
NM.distinctive_law: New Mexico NMSA §74-6-21 makes the New Mexico Department of Agriculture the residential pesticide applicator licensor with statewide preemption over municipal pesticide ordinances — and the New Mexico Office of the State Engineer additionally administers the most senior prior-appropriation residential outdoor water rights doctrine in the country, with Albuquerque Bernalillo County Water Utility Authority enforcing odd-even outdoor irrigation schedules and ABCWUA Time-of-Use rate tiering aggressively against high-volume outdoor irrigation users.
NM.climate_concern: New Mexico's high-altitude combination of 14-inch annual rainfall in Albuquerque, Las Cruces, and Roswell, intense UV exposure above 5,000 ft elevation in Santa Fe and Taos, and Rio Grande Compact prior-appropriation water rights doctrine forces every residential landscape install toward xeriscape, desert-adapted, and short-grass-prairie palettes — and Santa Fe and Taos historic-district adobe substrates require lime-amended bed prep and traditional Spanish Colonial Revival landscape design with desert willow, chamisa, apache plume, and cholla cactus replacing ornamental turf statewide.

NY.name: New York
NY.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; New York State Department of Environmental Conservation Pesticide Bureau; NYC Department of Consumer & Worker Protection HIC license
NY.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; NYC HIC license required above $200 contract within five boroughs; NYS DEC Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
NY.distinctive_law: New York Environmental Conservation Law §33 imposes the Neighbor Notification Law on every restricted-use pesticide application on residential property — every New York commercial landscape contractor must provide 48-hour advance written notification to neighbors within 150 ft of any restricted-use pesticide application, plus the NYS Child-Safe Playing Fields Act bans synthetic pesticide application on school and daycare property, and NYC Local Law 142 of 2021 separately bans non-essential synthetic pesticide application on every NYC park and city-maintained property, the most restrictive municipal pesticide ordinance in the Northeast.
NY.climate_concern: New York's combination of Adirondack and Catskill mountain forest-soil substrates upstate, dense suburban canopy across Westchester and Long Island, and Long Island Sound and Atlantic coastal salt-spray on every Long Island residential property drives Long Island Pine Barrens Commission native-plant overlay on every new install in the 100,000-acre core protected area — and Hudson Valley and Catskills landscape installs require terraced retaining walls and drainage swales on every sloping residential lot, doubling typical hardscape labor versus flat suburban subdivision installs in Long Island Nassau and Suffolk counties.

NC.name: North Carolina
NC.license_board_name: North Carolina Landscape Contractors' Licensing Board + North Carolina Department of Agriculture & Consumer Services Pesticide Section
NC.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; NCLCLB Landscape Contractor license required for any commercial landscape contracting above $30,000 contract; NCDACS Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
NC.distinctive_law: North Carolina General Statutes §89D makes North Carolina one of the only states with a dedicated Landscape Contractors' Licensing Board — every North Carolina commercial landscape contractor exceeding the $30,000 contract threshold must hold an NCLCLB license with separate exam categories for design, installation, and maintenance, plus a $25,000 surety bond on file, and the Board operates a Disciplinary Review Committee enforcing complaint inspections statewide on every licensed-contractor jobsite dispute.
NC.climate_concern: North Carolina's combination of red-clay subsoil across the Piedmont from Charlotte to Raleigh to Greensboro, mountain-substrate boulder beds across Asheville and Boone, and Atlantic Coastal Plain sandy soils across Wilmington and the Outer Banks creates one of the most variable residential landscape design environments in the Southeast — Piedmont installs require gypsum-amended clay subgrade prep, mountain installs require ripping plus 4-inch topsoil amendment, and coastal installs require salt-tolerant beach plant selection across the Cape Hatteras corridor.

ND.name: North Dakota
ND.license_board_name: North Dakota Department of Agriculture Pesticide & Fertilizer Program + North Dakota Secretary of State Contractor License
ND.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; NDDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; ND Contractor License required statewide above $4,000 contract
ND.distinctive_law: North Dakota Century Code §4.1-33 makes the North Dakota Department of Agriculture the residential pesticide applicator licensor and grants NDDA preemption authority over every municipal pesticide ordinance — North Dakota is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Northern Plains, blocking Fargo, Bismarck, and Grand Forks from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, while NDDA additionally operates the North Dakota Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting commercial sugar beet, sunflower, and canola operations adjacent to suburban subdivisions.
ND.climate_concern: North Dakota's Northern Plains combination of -40°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, 130-day growing season in interior climate zones 3a-4b, and 60-inch frost depth restricts residential turf to ultra-cold-hardy Kentucky bluegrass and creeping red fescue cool-season blends — and Bakken oil-patch fugitive dust contamination in the Williston, Tioga, and Watford City corridor measurably stresses ornamental flower beds and freshly-installed shade-tree saplings, with Western North Dakota landscape installs requiring imported topsoil + organic amendment on every new bed prep statewide.

OH.name: Ohio
OH.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; Ohio Department of Agriculture Pesticide Regulation; Cleveland, Columbus, Cincinnati municipal contractor credentials
OH.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; ODA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; municipal contractor registration required in Cleveland, Cincinnati, Columbus on residential exterior contracting above $5,000
OH.distinctive_law: Ohio Revised Code §921 grants the Ohio Department of Agriculture exclusive preemption authority over residential and municipal pesticide ordinances — Ohio is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Midwest, blocking Cleveland, Columbus, Cincinnati, and Toledo from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, with ODA Pesticide Regulation Section managing the only operational regulatory channel statewide and ODA additionally maintaining the Ohio Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting beekeeping operations and certified-organic Amish farms in Wayne, Holmes, and Stark counties.
OH.climate_concern: Ohio's combination of glacial-till loam soils across the Western Reserve and Allegheny Plateau, mature deciduous canopy across older Cleveland, Cincinnati, and Columbus suburbs, and 175-day growing season supports cool-season Kentucky bluegrass-perennial ryegrass blend turf as the dominant Ohio residential lawn — and Lake Erie lake-effect snow loads on Cleveland, Toledo, and Akron landscapes drive snow-shoveling-corridor design considerations on every front-yard install plus salt-tolerant plant selection on grade-level beds adjacent to ODOT snow-operations corridors.

OK.name: Oklahoma
OK.license_board_name: Oklahoma Department of Agriculture, Food & Forestry Consumer Protection Services — Pesticide Division
OK.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; ODAFF Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Oklahoma City and Tulsa municipal contractor registration required for residential exterior contracting above $5,000
OK.distinctive_law: Oklahoma 2 OS §3-81 grants the Oklahoma Department of Agriculture, Food & Forestry exclusive preemption authority over residential and municipal pesticide ordinances — Oklahoma is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Southern Plains, blocking Oklahoma City, Tulsa, and Norman from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, with ODAFF Pesticide Division managing the only operational regulatory channel statewide and ODAFF additionally maintaining the Oklahoma Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting commercial pecan, peach, and beekeeping operations adjacent to suburban subdivisions.
OK.climate_concern: Oklahoma's combination of Cross Timbers transitional substrates across Central Oklahoma, red-clay subsoil across the Anadarko Basin and Western Oklahoma, and 215-day growing season pushes Oklahoma residential landscape design toward warm-season Bermuda and zoysia turf with regular irrigation — and central-plains hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely shred ornamental flower beds and large-leaf shrubs across the entire state during the April-June severe weather season, plus Oklahoma red-clay dust contamination during high-wind spring events.

OR.name: Oregon
OR.license_board_name: Oregon Landscape Contractors Board + Oregon Construction Contractors Board + Oregon Department of Agriculture Pesticides Division
OR.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; OLCB Landscape Contracting Business license required for any commercial landscape contracting; ODA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
OR.distinctive_law: Oregon ORS §671 makes Oregon one of the only states with a dedicated Landscape Contractors Board — every Oregon commercial landscape contractor must hold an OLCB Landscape Contracting Business license with separate exam categories for design, installation, irrigation, and maintenance, plus a $20,000 surety bond on file, and the Board operates a Construction Contractors Board-coordinated complaint inspection program enforcing every residential landscape jobsite dispute statewide.
OR.climate_concern: Oregon's Willamette Valley combination of 215-day growing season, Pacific Northwest mildew-prone humidity, and Coast Range wood-substrate moisture cycling supports lush Pacific NW native plant palette with sword fern, Oregon grape, salal, and red-flowering currant on every new install — and Oregon's wildfire-driven WUI defensible-space framework under ORS §477 plus Oregon Department of Forestry advisory rules push residential landscape design toward fire-adapted plant selection in the Cascade and Klamath foothills, with juniper, manzanita, and other ladder-fuel species banned within 5 ft of structures in mapped fire-hazard zones.

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.license_board_name: Pennsylvania Department of Agriculture Bureau of Plant Industry — Pesticide Programs + Office of Attorney General HIC registration
PA.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; PDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; PA HIC registration required above $5,000 annual gross
PA.distinctive_law: Pennsylvania Pesticide Control Act (3 P.S. §111.21) makes the Pennsylvania Department of Agriculture the residential pesticide applicator licensor and grants PDA preemption authority over municipal pesticide ordinances — Pennsylvania blocks Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, Allentown, and Erie from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, but Pennsylvania separately operates the Chesapeake Bay Pollution Reduction Plan that caps commercial nitrogen fertilizer application rates on residential property in the Susquehanna River watershed under PA Act 38 nutrient management rules.
PA.climate_concern: Pennsylvania's combination of acidic forest-soil substrates across the Appalachian Plateau and Pocono Mountains, mature deciduous canopy across Philadelphia Main Line and Pittsburgh suburbs, and Susquehanna River watershed Chesapeake Bay TMDL nutrient-reduction targets pushes Pennsylvania residential landscape design toward bay-friendly natives, low-input cool-season tall fescue turf, and rain garden bioretention on every new install — and the state's pre-1900 housing-stock concentration in Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, Allentown, and Lancaster triggers lead-soil testing on landscape installs adjacent to pre-1978 painted exteriors.

RI.name: Rhode Island
RI.license_board_name: Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management Office of Agriculture + Rhode Island Contractors Registration & Licensing Board
RI.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; CRLB Contractor Registration required statewide regardless of contract value; RI DEM Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care
RI.distinctive_law: Rhode Island General Laws §23-25 imposes one of the most restrictive residential pesticide notification regimes in New England — every Rhode Island commercial landscape contractor applying restricted-use pesticides on residential property within 50 ft of an occupied structure must provide 24-hour advance written notification, plus Rhode Island's 2018 Healthy Soils Act under RI Gen. Laws §2-25.1 created statewide soil-health incentive programs targeting residential landscape contractor adoption of organic-matter-building bed prep and cover-cropping on every transitional residential install.
RI.climate_concern: Rhode Island's combination of acidic glacial-till soils across the Blackstone Valley and South County, mature deciduous canopy across East Side Providence and Newport, and Narragansett Bay watershed nutrient-management framework restricts standard high-nitrogen turf fertilizer programs across the state — and the state's sandy outwash soils across Block Island and the Newport coastal corridor combined with North Atlantic salt-spray on every Westerly to Newport residential property require salt-tolerant beach plum, bayberry, and northern bayberry hedging on every coastal install.

SC.name: South Carolina
SC.license_board_name: South Carolina Department of Pesticide Regulation (Clemson University) + South Carolina Department of Labor, Licensing & Regulation
SC.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; SC DPR Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; SC LLR residential license required above $5,000 contract
SC.distinctive_law: South Carolina Code §46-13 grants Clemson University's Department of Pesticide Regulation exclusive authority over residential pesticide regulation statewide — South Carolina is unusual in housing pesticide regulation under a land-grant university Department of Pesticide Regulation rather than a state Department of Agriculture, with Clemson DPR operating both the licensing program and the academic Cooperative Extension continuing-education channel for every South Carolina commercial landscape contractor applying chemicals on residential property statewide.
SC.climate_concern: South Carolina's combination of warm-humid Lowcountry climate from Charleston to Hilton Head, alluvial Coastal Plain soils across the Pee Dee region, and 240-day growing season supports near-year-round residential landscape demand on warm-season Bermuda, zoysia, and centipede turf with St. Augustinegrass dominant on coastal Lowcountry — and the state's pine-belt across the Sandhills supports loblolly and longleaf pine canopy that drives fungicide-rotation programs for take-all root rot on every residential turf install in the coastal corridor plus Lowcountry historic-district landscape design with palmetto, oak, and hydrangea palette dominating Charleston single-family residential properties.

SD.name: South Dakota
SD.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; South Dakota Department of Agriculture & Natural Resources Pesticide Section
SD.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; SD DANR Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; municipal contractor registration required in Sioux Falls and Rapid City
SD.distinctive_law: South Dakota Codified Laws §38-21 makes the South Dakota Department of Agriculture & Natural Resources the residential pesticide applicator licensor and grants SD DANR preemption authority over municipal pesticide ordinances — South Dakota is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Northern Plains, blocking Sioux Falls, Rapid City, and Pierre from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, while SD DANR additionally maintains the South Dakota Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting commercial sunflower, soybean, and beekeeping operations adjacent to suburban subdivisions.
SD.climate_concern: South Dakota's Black Hills combination of 145-day growing season in interior climate zone 4a-5a, intense Black Hills high-altitude UV exposure above 4,000 ft elevation, and 20-inch annual rainfall supports xeriscape, native-prairie, and short-grass perennial palettes as the dominant western South Dakota residential landscape aesthetic — while Eastern South Dakota Sioux Falls supports cool-season Kentucky bluegrass turf, and Black Hills wildfire-smoke seasons since 2017 measurably stress freshly-installed shade-tree saplings, with imported topsoil + organic amendment standard on every new bed prep across Rapid City, Spearfish, and Sturgis.

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.license_board_name: Tennessee Department of Agriculture Pesticide Section + Tennessee Board for Licensing Contractors
TN.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; TDA Charter Pest Control Operator required for any commercial landscape pesticide application; TN BLC contractor license required above $25,000 contract
TN.distinctive_law: Tennessee Code §43-19 makes the Tennessee Department of Agriculture the residential pesticide applicator licensor under a unique Charter Pest Control Operator framework — every Tennessee commercial landscape contractor applying any pesticide on residential property must hold TDA Charter Pest Control Operator credentials with separate categories for ornamental, turf, and right-of-way application, plus mandatory Tennessee Pesticide Liability Insurance disclosure to every residential customer, the most restrictive insurance disclosure regime in the Southeast.
TN.climate_concern: Tennessee's transition zone between Northern cool-season and Southern warm-season turf grasses creates one of the most variable residential landscape design environments in the South — Eastern Tennessee Knoxville supports cool-season tall fescue and Kentucky bluegrass, while Western Tennessee Memphis and Jackson default to warm-season Bermuda and zoysia, with Middle Tennessee Nashville and Murfreesboro requiring transition-zone overseed schedules combining spring Bermuda greenup with fall fescue overseeding on every premium residential install.

TX.name: Texas
TX.license_board_name: Texas Department of Agriculture Pesticide Programs + Texas Department of Licensing & Regulation Landscape Architect
TX.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; TDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; HOA approval typical in deed-restricted communities
TX.distinctive_law: Texas HB 645 (2013) grants every Texas homeowner the statutory right to install drought-tolerant native plants and xeriscape regardless of HOA covenants, restrictions, or deed-recorded landscape requirements — overriding HOA mandates in deed-restricted communities and forcing every Texas HOA architectural review committee to approve native xeriscape applications meeting Texas Department of Agriculture-published low-water-use plant lists, the strongest residential xeriscape-protection statute in the country alongside Florida's FFL Act.
TX.climate_concern: Texas's dramatic east-west climate variation from East Texas piney woods (52-inch annual rainfall, 8a hardiness) to Trans-Pecos arid (8-inch annual rainfall, 7a-8a hardiness) drives state-specific residential landscape design — Houston and Beaumont support St. Augustinegrass turf with Gulf-coastal humidity, Dallas-Fort Worth supports Bermuda and zoysia with regular irrigation, while West Texas El Paso, Lubbock, and Amarillo restrict residential turf to drought-tolerant buffalograss native cultivars and desert-adapted xeriscape palettes.

UT.name: Utah
UT.license_board_name: Utah Department of Agriculture & Food Pesticide Section + Utah Division of Occupational and Professional Licensing (S320 Landscape Contractor)
UT.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; UDAF Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; DOPL S320 Landscape Contractor specialty license required statewide on contracts above $3,000
UT.distinctive_law: Utah Code §73-10g imposes the Utah Water Quality Act statewide turf-grass conversion incentive program — Utah Division of Water Resources administers the $1.50-per-square-foot Localscapes turf-replacement rebate fund for residential conversion of irrigated cool-season turf to native or low-water perennial beds, with Utah additionally mandating that new state-funded municipal projects install no more than 20% turf area, the most restrictive state-level turf-area cap in the Mountain West.
UT.climate_concern: Utah's combination of 13-inch annual rainfall in the Wasatch Front, intense Salt Lake City and Provo high-altitude UV exposure above 4,500 ft elevation, and Great Salt Lake watershed water-rights pressure has driven aggressive Localscapes-program adoption converting cool-season Kentucky bluegrass turf to drought-tolerant native plant palettes statewide — and Park City and Deer Valley high-altitude exposure above 7,000 ft restricts residential plant selection to alpine perennial cultivars with extreme cold-hardiness on every premium ski-town residential install.

VT.name: Vermont
VT.license_board_name: Vermont Agency of Agriculture, Food & Markets Pesticide Programs + Vermont Office of Professional Regulation Residential Contractor Registration
VT.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; VAAFM Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; Vermont Residential Contractor Registration required above $10,000 contract
VT.distinctive_law: Vermont 6 V.S.A. §1102 imposes the most restrictive residential neonicotinoid ban in the country — every Vermont commercial landscape contractor applying neonicotinoid-class pesticides on residential property must satisfy enhanced pollinator-protection certification under the Vermont Pollinator Protection Committee framework, plus Vermont Act 110 (2022) banned chlorpyrifos statewide for all residential landscape uses ahead of the federal EPA tolerance revocation, the most restrictive Northeast state on residential pesticide active-ingredient restrictions.
VT.climate_concern: Vermont's combination of 145-day growing season in interior climate zone 4a-5a, acidic Green Mountain forest-soil substrates across the entire state, and 60-inch frost depth restricts residential lawn establishment to specialty cool-season fine fescue and creeping red fescue blends — and Vermont's high mature-deciduous canopy across the Green Mountains and Lake Champlain Valley plus intense fall-foliage tannin drip on east-facing exterior elevations measurably stresses ornamental flower beds, with Vermont native-plant palette featuring sugar maple, paper birch, and red trillium dominating premium residential installs.

VA.name: Virginia
VA.license_board_name: Virginia Department of Agriculture & Consumer Services Office of Pesticide Services + Virginia Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation
VA.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; VDACS Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; DPOR Class C Contractor license required above $1,000 contract
VA.distinctive_law: Virginia Code §3.2-3939.1 imposes the Virginia Lawn Fertilizer & Lawn Maintenance Code statewide — every Virginia commercial lawn-care contractor must apply zero phosphorus on established turf except at establishment, comply with seasonal blackout periods on phosphorus and nitrogen application, and post mandatory contractor signage detailing application content, all to protect Chesapeake Bay TMDL nutrient-reduction targets, with VDACS enforcement targeting commercial landscape contractor compliance through residential complaint inspections.
VA.climate_concern: Virginia's combination of Chesapeake Bay watershed-protection nutrient caps under the Lawn Fertilizer Law, mature deciduous canopy across the Northern Virginia I-95 corridor and Tidewater, and 200-day growing season pushes Virginia residential landscape design toward bay-friendly natives, low-input cool-season tall fescue turf, and rain garden bioretention on every new install — and Virginia's Tidewater coastal salt-spray plus the Virginia Beach Coastal Erosion Control Zone restrict shoreline residential landscaping to salt-tolerant beach plum, sea oats, and bayberry hedging on every coastal Virginia property.

WA.name: Washington
WA.license_board_name: Washington State Department of Agriculture Pesticide Management Division + Washington State Department of Labor & Industries Contractor Registration
WA.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; WSDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; L&I Contractor Registration required statewide regardless of contract value
WA.distinctive_law: Washington RCW 17.21 imposes the most restrictive residential pesticide notification regime in the Pacific Northwest — every Washington commercial landscape contractor must register with WSDA as a Commercial Pesticide Applicator, satisfy enhanced pollinator-protection requirements on residential property within 1/2 mile of registered apiaries, and Washington Growth Management Act §36.70A separately requires every new residential landscape install in mapped Critical Aquifer Recharge Areas to comply with WSDOE rain garden bioretention design standards.
WA.climate_concern: Washington's Puget Sound combination of 38-inch annual rainfall concentrated in the October-May wet season, persistent Pacific Northwest mildew growing conditions, and Cascade Range wood-substrate moisture cycling supports lush Pacific NW native plant palette with sword fern, Oregon grape, salal, and red-flowering currant on every Seattle, Bellevue, Tacoma, and Olympia install — and Washington Coast salt-spray on Aberdeen, Long Beach, and Anacortes residential properties mandates salt-tolerant beach plum, sea oats, and bayberry hedging on every coastal install.

WV.name: West Virginia
WV.license_board_name: West Virginia Department of Agriculture Pesticide Regulatory Programs + West Virginia Division of Labor Contractor Licensing Board
WV.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; WVDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; WV Division of Labor Contractor License required above $2,500 contract
WV.distinctive_law: West Virginia Code §19-16A grants the West Virginia Department of Agriculture exclusive preemption authority over residential and municipal pesticide ordinances — West Virginia is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Appalachian region, blocking Charleston, Huntington, Wheeling, and Morgantown from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, with WVDA Pesticide Regulatory Programs managing the only operational regulatory channel statewide and WVDA additionally maintaining the West Virginia Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting commercial apple, peach, and beekeeping operations.
WV.climate_concern: West Virginia's Appalachian mountain combination of acidic forest-soil substrates across the Allegheny Plateau, mountain-substrate boulder beds across Charleston and Beckley, and 175-day growing season restricts residential lawn establishment to shade-tolerant cool-season fine fescue blends — and West Virginia's mountainous topography requires terraced retaining walls and drainage swales on every sloping residential lot, doubling typical hardscape labor versus flat suburban subdivision installs and pushing typical West Virginia residential landscape installs toward forest-edge native rhododendron, mountain laurel, and dogwood palettes.

WI.name: Wisconsin
WI.license_board_name: Wisconsin Department of Agriculture, Trade & Consumer Protection Pesticide Section + Wisconsin Department of Safety & Professional Services Dwelling Contractor
WI.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; DATCP Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; WI Dwelling Contractor Certification required for any one- and two-family dwelling exterior contracting
WI.distinctive_law: Wisconsin Statutes §94.701 grants the Wisconsin Department of Agriculture, Trade & Consumer Protection exclusive preemption authority over residential and municipal pesticide ordinances — Wisconsin is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Upper Midwest, blocking Milwaukee, Madison, Green Bay, and Eau Claire from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, with DATCP Pesticide Section managing the only operational regulatory channel statewide and DATCP additionally maintaining the Wisconsin Sensitive-Crop Registry protecting commercial cranberry, apple, and beekeeping operations.
WI.climate_concern: Wisconsin's combination of glacial-till loam soils across the Driftless Area and Door County, mature deciduous canopy across older Milwaukee and Madison neighborhoods, and 160-day growing season supports cool-season Kentucky bluegrass-perennial ryegrass blend turf as the dominant Wisconsin residential lawn — and Wisconsin's Lake Michigan and Lake Superior lake-effect snow loads on Milwaukee, Green Bay, and Superior landscapes drive snow-shoveling-corridor design considerations on every front-yard install plus salt-tolerant plant selection on grade-level beds adjacent to Wisconsin DOT snow-operations corridors.

WY.name: Wyoming
WY.license_board_name: No statewide landscape license; Wyoming Department of Agriculture Technical Services Pesticide Program
WY.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; WDA Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; municipal contractor registration required in Cheyenne and Jackson
WY.distinctive_law: Wyoming Statute §35-7 makes the Wyoming Department of Agriculture the residential pesticide applicator licensor and grants WDA preemption authority over every municipal pesticide ordinance — Wyoming is one of the strongest pesticide-preemption states in the Mountain West, blocking Cheyenne, Casper, Jackson, and Laramie from adopting local pesticide restrictions on residential landscape contractors, while WDA additionally operates the Wyoming Noxious Weed and Pest Control Program providing cost-share for residential property owners managing leafy spurge, knapweed, and Russian olive infestations.
WY.climate_concern: Wyoming's high-altitude combination of 130-day growing season in interior climate zones 3a-5a, the highest-elevation residential development in the continental U.S. across Jackson Hole and Cody at 6,000+ ft elevation, and 13-inch annual rainfall supports xeriscape, native shortgrass-prairie, and short-grass perennial palettes as the dominant Wyoming residential landscape aesthetic — and chinook wind events plus Bighorn Basin and Powder River Basin oil-and-gas patch fugitive dust contamination measurably stress freshly-installed shade-tree saplings and ornamental flower beds, with native sagebrush and Great Basin grasses dominating premium Wyoming residential installs.

DC.name: District of Columbia
DC.license_board_name: DC Department of Energy & Environment Pesticide Branch + DC Department of Licensing & Consumer Protection HIC license
DC.permit_typical: no permit required for landscape install; DOEE Commercial Pesticide Applicator required for any chemical lawn care; DC HIC license required above $300 contract
DC.distinctive_law: District of Columbia Pesticide Education and Control Amendment Act of 2012 (DC Law 19-191) imposes the strictest municipal residential pesticide regime in the country — every DC commercial landscape contractor applying restricted-use pesticides on residential property within 25 ft of an occupied structure must provide 24-hour advance written notification, plus DOEE separately bans the use of glyphosate, atrazine, and 2,4-D on every DC park and city-maintained property under the DC Pesticide-Free Parks Act, and DC requires every commercial landscape contractor to provide an Integrated Pest Management Plan to every residential customer.
DC.climate_concern: District of Columbia's combination of Chesapeake Bay watershed-protection nutrient caps under the DC Stormwater Management Regulations, mature deciduous canopy across Capitol Hill, Georgetown, Cleveland Park, and Tenleytown, and 210-day growing season pushes DC residential landscape design toward bay-friendly natives, low-input cool-season tall fescue turf, and rain garden bioretention on every new install — and DC's pre-1978 housing-stock concentration triggers lead-soil testing on landscape installs adjacent to pre-1978 painted exteriors across the majority of DC single-family residential rowhouse stock.
